title: Moser Flow: Divergence-based Generative Modeling on Manifolds [PDF]

## **Moser Flow: Divergence-based Generative Modeling on Manifolds**

**Noam Rozen**[1] **Aditya Grover**[2] _[,]_[3] **Maximilian Nickel**[2] **Yaron Lipman**[1] _[,]_[2] 1Weizmann Institute of Science 2Facebook AI Research 3UCLA

## **Abstract**

We are interested in learning generative models for complex geometries described via manifolds, such as spheres, tori, and other implicit surfaces. Current extensions of existing (Euclidean) generative models are restricted to specific geometries and typically suffer from high computational costs. We introduce _Moser Flow_ (MF), a new class of generative models within the family of continuous normalizing flows (CNF). MF also produces a CNF via a solution to the change-of-variable formula, however differently from other CNF methods, its model (learned) density is parameterized as the source (prior) density minus the _divergence_ of a neural network (NN). The divergence is a local, linear differential operator, easy to approximate and calculate on manifolds. Therefore, unlike other CNFs, MF does not require invoking or backpropagating through an ODE solver during training. Furthermore, representing the model density explicitly as the divergence of a NN rather than as a solution of an ODE facilitates learning high fidelity densities. Theoretically, we prove that MF constitutes a universal density approximator under suitable assumptions. Empirically, we demonstrate for the first time the use of flow models for sampling from general curved surfaces and achieve significant improvements in density estimation, sample quality, and training complexity over existing CNFs on challenging synthetic geometries and real-world benchmarks from the earth and climate sciences.

## **1 Introduction**

The major successes of deep generative models in recent years are primarily in domains involving Euclidean data, such as images (Dhariwal and Nichol, 2021), text (Brown et al., 2020), and video (Kumar et al., 2019). However, many kinds of scientific data in the real world lie in non-Euclidean spaces specified as manifolds. Examples include planetary-scale data for earth and climate sciences (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020), protein interactions and brain imaging data for life sciences (Gerber et al., 2010; Chen et al., 2012), as well as 3D shapes in computer graphics (Hoppe et al., 1992; Kazhdan et al., 2006). Existing (Euclidean) generative models cannot be effectively applied in these scenarios as they would tend to assign some probability mass to areas outside the natural geometry of these domains.

An effective way to impose geometric domain constraints for deep generative modeling is to design _normalizing flows_ that operate in the desired manifold space. A normalizing flow maps a prior (source) distribution to a target distribution via the change of variables formula (Rezende and Mohamed, 2015; Dinh et al., 2016; Papamakarios et al., 2019). Early work in this direction proposed invertible architectures for learning probability distributions directly over the specific manifolds defined over spheres and tori (Rezende et al., 2020). Recently, Mathieu and Nickel (2020) proposed to extend continuous normalizing flows (CNF) (Chen et al., 2018) for generative modeling over Riemannian manifolds wherein the flows are defined via vector fields on manifolds and computed as the solution to an associated ordinary differential equation (ODE). CNFs have the advantage that the neural

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

network architectures parameterizing the flow need not be restricted via invertibility constraints. However, as we show in our experiments, existing CNFs such as FFJORD (Grathwohl et al., 2018) and Riemannian CNFs (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020) can be slow to converge and the generated samples can be inferior in capturing the details of high fidelity data densities. Moreover, it is a real challenge to apply Riemannian CNFs to complex geometries such as general curved surfaces.

To address these challenges, we propose Moser Flows (MF), a new class of deep generative models within the CNF family. An MF models the desired target density as the source density minus the divergence of an (unrestricted) neural network. The divergence is a local, linear differential operator, easy to approximate and calculate on manifolds. By drawing on classic results in differential geometry by Moser (1965) and Dacorogna and Moser (1990), we can show that this parameterization induces a CNF solution to the change-of-variables formula specified via an ODE. Since MFs directly parameterize the model density using the divergence, unlike other CNF methods, we do not require to explicitly solve the ODE for maximum likelihood training. At test-time, we use the ODE solver for generation. We derive extensions to MFs for Euclidean submanifolds that efficiently parameterize vector fields projected to the desired manifold domain. Theoretically, we prove that Moser Flows are universal generative models over Euclidean submanifolds. That is, given a Euclidean submanifold _M_ and a target continuous positive probability density _µ_ over _M_ , MFs can push arbitrary positive source density _ν_ over _M_ to densities _µ_ ¯ that are arbitrarily close to _µ_ .

We evaluate Moser Flows on a wide range of challenging real and synthetic problems defined over many different domains. On synthetic problems, we demonstrate improvements in convergence speed for attaining a desired level of details in generation quality. We then experiment with two kinds of complex geometries. First, we show significant improvements of 49% on average over Riemannian CNFs (Mathieu and Nickel, 2020) for density estimation as well as high-fidelity generation on 4 earth and climate science datasets corresponding to global locations of volcano eruptions, earthquakes, floods, and wildfires on spherical geometries. Next and last, we go beyond spherical geometries to demonstrate for the first time, generative models on general curved surfaces.

## **3 Moser Flow**

Moser (1965) and Dacorogna and Moser (1990) suggested a method for solving the normalizing equation, that is equation 2. Their method explicitly constructs a vector field _vt_ , and the flow it defines via equation 3 is guaranteed to solve equation 2. We start by introducing the method, adapted to our needs, followed by its application to generative modeling. We will use notations introduced above.

## **5 Experiments**

In all experiments, we modeled a manifold vector field as a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) _**u** θ ∈_ X( _M_ ), with parameters _θ_ . All models were trained using Adam optimizer (Kingma and Ba, 2014), and in all neural networks the activation is Softplus with _β_ = 100. Unless stated otherwise, we set _λ_ + = 0. We used an exact calculation of the divergence div _E_ ( _**u**_ ( _**x**_ )). We experimented with two kinds of manifolds.

**Flat Torus.** To test our method on Euclidean 2D data, we used _M_ as the flat torus, that is the unit square [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1][2] with opposite edges identified. This defines a manifold with no boundary which is locally isometric to the Euclidean plane. Due to this local isometry the Riemannian divergence on the flat

## **5.1 Toy distributions**

First, we consider a collection of challenging toy 2D datasets explored in prior works (Chen et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2021). We scale samples to lie in the flat torus [ _−_ 1 _,_ 1][2] and use _k_ = 1 for the positional encoding. Figure 2 depicts the input data samples, the generated samples _after_ training, and the learned distribution _µ_ ¯. In the top six datasets, the MLP architecture used for Moser Flows consists of 3 hidden layers with 256 units each, whereas in the bottom two we used 4 hidden layers with 256 neurons due to the higher complexity of these distributions. We set _λ−_ = 2.

## **5.3 Time evaluations**

To compare our method to Euclidean CNF methods, we compare Moser Flow with FFJORD (Grathwohl et al., 2018) on the flat torus. We consider a challenging density with high frequencies obtained via 800x600 images (Figure 4). We generate a new batch every iteration by sampling each pixel location with probability which is proportional to the pixel intensity. The architectures of both _**v** θ_ and the vector field defined in FFJORD are the same, namely an MLP with 4 hidden layers of 256 neurons each. To capture the higher frequencies in the image we use a positional encoding with _k_ = 8 for both methods. We used a batch size of 10k. We used learning rate of 1e-5 for Moser Flow and 1e-4 for FFJORD. We used _λ−_ = 2. Learning was stopped after 5k seconds. Figure 4 presents the results. Note that Moser Flow captures high-frequency details better than FFJORD. This is also expressed in the graph on the top right, showing how the NLL decreases faster for MF than FFJORD. ## **5.5 Curved surfaces**

We trained an SDF _f_ for the Stanford Bunny surface _M_ using the method in Gropp et al. (2020). To generate uniform ( _ν_ ) and data ( _µ_ ) samples over _M_ we first extract a mesh _M[′]_ from _f_ using the Marching Cubes algorithm (Lorensen and Cline, 1987) setting its resolution to 100x100x100. Then, to randomly choose a point uniformly from _M[′]_ we first randomly choose a face of the mesh with probability proportional to its area, and then randomly choose a point uniformly within that face. For target _µ_ we used clamped manifold harmonics to create a sequence of densities with increased complexity. To that end, we first computed the _k_ -th eigenfunction of the Laplace-Beltrami operator over _M[′]_ (we provide details on this computation in the supplementary), for the frequencies (eigenvalues) _k ∈{_ 10 _,_ 50 _,_ 500 _}_ . Next, we sampled the eigenfunctions at the faces’ centers, clamped their negative values, and normalized to get discrete probability densities over the faces of _M[′]_ . Then, to sample a point, we first choose a face at random based on this probability, and then random a point uniformly within that face. We take 500k i.i.d. samples of this distribution as our dataset. We take _**v** θ_ to be an MLP with 6 hidden layers of dimension 512. We use batch size of 10k for both the NLL loss and for the integral approximation; we ran for 1000 epochs with learning rate of 1e-4. We used _λ−_ = _λ_ + = 1. Figure 6 depict the results. Note that Moser Flow is able to learn the surface densities for all three frequencies.

## **6 Related Work**

In the following, we discuss related work on normalizing flows for manifold-valued data. On a high level, such methods can be divided into _Parametric_ versus _Riemannian_ methods. Parametric methods consist of a normalizing flow in the Euclidean space R _[n]_ , pushed-forward onto the manifold through an invertible map _ψ_ : R _[n] →M_ . However, to globally represent the manifold, _ψ_ needs to be a homeomorphism implying that _M_ and R _[n]_ are topologically equivalent, limiting the scope of that

approach. Existing methods in this class are often based on the exponential map exp _x_ : _TxM_ = _[∼]_ R _[n] →M_ of a manifold. This leads to the so called _wrapped_ distributions. This approach has been taken, for instance, by Falorsi et al. (2019) and Bose et al. (2020) to parametrize probability distributions on Lie groups and hyperbolic space. However, Parametric methods based on the exponential map often lead to numerical and computational challenges. For instance, in compact manifolds (e.g., spheres or the SO(3) group) computing the density of _wrapped_ distributions requires an infinite summation. On the hyperboloid, on the other hand, the exponential map is numerically not well-behaved far away from the origin (Dooley and Wildberger, 1993; Al-Mohy and Higham, 2010).

In contrast to Parametric methods, Riemannian methods operate directly on the manifold itself and, as such, avoid numerical instabilities that arise from the mapping onto the manifold. Early work in this class of models proposed transformations along geodesics on the hypersphere by evaluating the exponential map at the gradient of a scalar manifold function (Sei, 2011). Rezende et al. (2020) introduced _discrete Riemannian_ flows for hyperspheres and torii based on Möbius transformations and spherical splines. Mathieu and Nickel (2020) introduced _continuous_ flows on general Riemannian manifolds (RCNF). In contrast to discrete flows (e.g., Bose et al., 2020; Rezende et al., 2020), such time-continuous flows alleviate the previous topological constraints by parametrizing the flow as the solution to an ODE over the manifold (Grathwohl et al., 2018). Concurrently to RCNF, Lou et al. (2020) and Falorsi and Forré (2020) proposed related extensions of neural ODEs to smooth manifolds. Moser Flow also generates a CNF, however by limiting the flow space (albeit, not the generated distributions) it allows expressing the learned distribution as the divergence of a vector field.

## **7 Discussion and limitations**

We introduced Moser Flow, a generative model in the family of CNFs that represents the target density using the divergence operator applied to a vector valued neural network. The main benefits of MF stems from the simplicity and locality of the divergence operator. MFs circumvent the need to solve an ODE in the training process, and are thus applicable on a broad class of manifolds. Theoretically, we prove MF is a universal generative model, able to (approximately) generate arbitrary positive target densities from arbitrary positive prior densities. Empirically, we show MF enjoys favorable computational speed in comparison to previous CNF models, improves density estimation on spherical data compared to previous work by a large margin, and for the first time facilitate training a CNF over a general curved surface.

One important future work direction, and a current limitation, is scaling of MF to higher dimensions. This challenge can be roughly broken to three parts: First, the model probabilities _µ_ ¯ should be computed/approximated in log-scale, as probabilities are expected to decrease exponentially with the dimension. Second, the variance of the numerical approximations of the integral � _M[µ]_[¯] _[−][dV]_ will increase significantly in high dimensions and needs to be controlled. Third, the divergence term, div( _u_ ), is too costly to be computed exactly in high dimensions and needs to be approximated, similarly to other CNF approaches. Finally, our work suggests a novel generative model, and similarly to other generative models can be potentially used for generation of fake data and amplify harmful biases in the dataset. Mitigating such harms is an active and important area of ongoing research.

### High-Level Feedback

This paper presents Moser Flow (MF), a novel and theoretically well-motivated approach to generative modeling on Riemannian manifolds that cleverly leverages classical results from differential geometry (Moser, 1965; Dacorogna and Moser, 1990) to parameterize densities via the divergence of a neural network, thereby circumventing the need to backpropagate through an ODE solver during training—a significant and practically impactful contribution. The introduction is generally well-written, motivates the problem clearly with compelling application domains (earth/climate sciences, curved surfaces), and makes concrete claims about theoretical universality and empirical improvements (e.g., a reported 49% average gain over Riemannian CNFs). However, several issues merit closer scrutiny: (1) the claim that MF avoids ODE solvers during training is somewhat overstated given that sampling/generation still requires solving an ODE, and the paper should more carefully discuss how the mismatch between the training objective (divergence-based density) and the test-time ODE-based sampling affects practical fidelity; (2) the parameterization requires enforcing positivity of the learned density (source minus divergence), which is a nontrivial constraint whose handling is not previewed in the introduction and could be a key source of optimization difficulty; (3) the universality theorem is stated only for Euclidean submanifolds, and the introduction would benefit from clarifying what assumptions underlie this result and whether it extends to intrinsic manifolds; and (4) comparisons should ideally include wall-clock training time and sample-quality metrics with proper statistical significance, as the current framing of "significant improvements" is vague. Finally, while the writing is mostly clear, the technical exposition in the preliminaries is dense and could benefit from a simple running illustration (e.g., on the sphere) to build intuition before the general Riemannian formalism.

progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and potentially important contribution: the motivation is compelling, the proposed divergence-based parameterization is elegant, and the connection to Moser/Dacorogna–Moser theory gives the method a solid geometric foundation while addressing a real limitation of CNFs on manifolds—costly ODE-based likelihood training. The introduction is generally clear and positions the work well against manifold normalizing flows and Riemannian CNFs, and the claimed empirical scope is ambitious and relevant. The most significant issues to scrutinize are technical and practical: it is not yet clear from the opening how the model guarantees positivity of the learned density when written as “source density minus divergence of a neural network,” even though normalization follows naturally on compact boundaryless manifolds; the assumptions of compactness, no boundary, orientability, and strictly positive densities may limit applicability and should be discussed prominently; and the claimed computational advantage depends on whether divergence evaluation/projection on general surfaces is indeed cheaper and stable in practice. The paper should also be careful with broad novelty claims such as “for the first time” unless prior work on flows, diffusion/score models, or surface-based density modeling is fully ruled out. Overall, the beginning suggests a high-quality, theoretically motivated paper with clear potential impact, but its persuasiveness will hinge on rigorous treatment of positivity and approximation constraints, fair empirical comparisons to ODE-based CNFs, and a transparent discussion of geometric assumptions and limitations.

The construction only yields a valid Moser-flow CNF when the learned density \( ar\mu=
u-\operatorname{div}u\) is strictly positive, so that the interpolant \((1-t)
u+t ar\mu\) remains positive and the velocity field \(u/((1-t)

comments.quote: Moser’s approach to solving equation 2 starts by interpolating the source and target distributions. That is, choosing an interpolant _αt_ : [0 _,_ 1] _× M →_ R _>_ 0, such that _α_ 0 = _ν_ , _α_ 1 = _µ_ , and  _M[α][t][dV]_[= 1] for all _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1]. For arbitrary continuous densities, the right-hand side of the divergence equation need not be the divergence of a smooth vector field exactly, and the resulting ODE may not define the claimed smooth diffeomorphism. However, the paragraph first defines the method class using an invertible map \(\psi\), then presents exponential-map wrapped distributions as examples. On compact manifolds such as spheres or \(SO(3)\), the exponential map is generally not globally injective, and wrapped densities are often computed by summing over multiple preimages. comments.title: Riemannian methods are said to avoid mapping-induced numerical instabilities too broadly
comments.quote: In contrast to Parametric methods, Riemannian methods operate directly on the manifold itself and, as such, avoid numerical instabilities that arise from the mapping onto the manifold.
comments.explanation: The contrast is directionally reasonable, since intrinsic methods can avoid singularities of a single global Euclidean parametrization. But the statement is too broad: many Riemannian methods still use exponential maps, logarithmic maps, retractions, charts, projections, or manifold-specific ODE solvers, each of which can introduce numerical issues. The claim should be narrowed to say that such methods avoid the particular global-parametrization instabilities of Euclidean push-forward approaches.

comments.title: Claim that limiting the flow space does not limit generated distributions needs theorem qualifiers
comments.quote: Moser Flow also generates a CNF, however by limiting the flow space (albeit, not the generated distributions) it allows expressing the learned distribution as the divergence of a vector field.
comments.explanation: The parenthetical “not the generated distributions” could be read as an exact, unrestricted expressivity claim. The paper’s support for this claim is qualified: it concerns positive densities under compactness/orientability/boundary assumptions, and in the neural parameterized setting it is an approximation/universality result rather than exact finite-network representability. Adding a qualifier such as “under the assumptions of our universality theorem” or “up to arbitrary approximation for positive continuous densities” would make the claim precise.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and potentially important contribution: the motivation is compelling, the proposed divergence-based parameterization is elegant, and the connection to Moser/Dacorogna–Moser theory gives the method a solid geometric foundation while addressing a real limitation of CNFs on manifolds—costly ODE-based likelihood training. The introduction is generally clear and positions the work well against manifold normalizing flows and Riemannian CNFs, and the claimed empirical scope is ambitious and relevant. The most significant issues to scrutinize are technical and practical: it is not yet clear from the opening how the model guarantees positivity of the learned density when written as “source density minus divergence of a neural network,” even though normalization follows naturally on compact boundaryless manifolds; the assumptions of compactness, no boundary, orientability, and strictly positive densities may limit applicability and should be discussed prominently; and the claimed computational advantage depends on whether divergence evaluation/projection on general surfaces is indeed cheaper and stable in practice. The paper should also be careful with broad novelty claims such as “for the first time” unless prior work on flows, diffusion/score models, or surface-based density modeling is fully ruled out. Overall, the beginning suggests a high-quality, theoretically motivated paper with clear potential impact, but its persuasiveness will hinge on rigorous treatment of positivity and approximation constraints, fair empirical comparisons to ODE-based CNFs, and a transparent discussion of geometric assumptions and limitations.

comments.title: CNF existence is stated without the required positivity qualification
comments.quote: MF also produces a CNF via a solution to the change-of-variable formula, however differently from other CNF methods, its model (learned) density is parameterized as the source (prior) density minus the _divergence_ of a neural network (NN).
comments.explanation:
I understand the intended high-level message: by parameterizing the learned density as \( ar\mu=
u-\operatorname{div}u\), the authors can invoke the Moser/Dacorogna--Moser construction to obtain a flow without training through an ODE solver. The later context does make clear an important condition: Corollary 1 only gives a probability density and a CNF if \( ar\mu>0\) on \(M\), and the practical loss only penalizes violations of a lower bound rather than enforcing positivity exactly. This matters because \(
u-\operatorname{div}u\) has unit integral under the stated boundary assumptions, but it need not be nonnegative. If it is sign-changing, the interpolant \((1-t)
u+t ar\mu\) can fail to be positive, the velocity field \(u/((1-t)
u+t ar\mu)\) may be ill-defined, and the Moser flow argument no longer applies. Thus the abstract/intro wording slightly overstates the guarantee: MF produces a CNF only for parameter choices satisfying the positivity condition, not for an arbitrary learned neural vector field.

comments.title: Regularity assumptions on densities appear weaker than what the Moser-flow construction normally requires
comments.quote: We consider _probability measures_ over _M_ that are represented by strictly positive continuous density functions _µ, ν_ : _M →_ R _>_ 0
comments.explanation: For defining probability measures on a compact Riemannian manifold, strictly positive continuous densities are perfectly adequate. My concern is with the later use of these objects in the Moser construction, where one solves a divergence equation and obtains a smooth time-dependent vector field whose ODE flow is a diffeomorphism. Classical Moser-type theorems are usually stated for smooth positive volume forms, or at least with sufficient \(C^k\) regularity to obtain a correspondingly regular vector field and flow. The paper later proves approximation of continuous target densities, so I can see why the authors want continuity as the statistical target class. But as written in the preliminaries, the same \(\mu,\nu\) notation seems to feed directly into the exact normalizing-flow construction. Unless the supplement explicitly separates the smooth case for the theorem from the continuous case for approximation, the stated assumptions are too weak for the claimed smooth CNF/diffeomorphism machinery.

comments.title: Moser theorem is applied without stating the needed smoothness regularity
comments.quote: Moser’s approach to solving equation 2 starts by interpolating the source and target distributions. That is, choosing an interpolant _αt_ : [0 _,_ 1] _× M →_ R _>_ 0, such that _α_ 0 = _ν_ , _α_ 1 = _µ_ , and  _M[α][t][dV]_[= 1] for all _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1]. Then, a time-dependent vector field _vt ∈_ X( _M_ ) is defined so that for each time _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1], the flow Φ _t_ defined by equation 3 satisfies the _continuous normalization equation_ :
comments.explanation: The overall construction is standard Moser: interpolate the volume forms, solve a divergence equation, and define a time-dependent vector field whose flow pulls back the interpolated volume form to the source. What made me pause is that the preliminaries define the densities as strictly positive continuous functions, while the argument here invokes smooth vector fields, a PDE of the form div(u_t)=... and a diffeomorphic ODE flow. In the classical Moser theorem, the volume forms are smooth, and the resulting vector field and flow are smooth; similarly, solving div(u_t)=h with u_t smooth requires h to have corresponding smoothness. For an arbitrary continuous target density, the right-hand side need not be the divergence of a smooth vector field exactly, and the induced ODE vector field may not be smooth enough to guarantee a diffeomorphism in the stated sense. This may be harmless if the intended statement is first for smooth densities and later extended by approximation, and the later universality theorem indeed seems approximation-oriented. But as written, Theorem 1 is presented as applying directly under the paper’s earlier continuous-density assumptions. A regularity qualifier such as “for smooth positive densities/interpolants” or an explicit approximation argument would remove the gap.

comments.title: Divergence formula appears to assume a global orthonormal frame
comments.quote: where _{ei}[n] i_ =1[is][an][orthonormal][frame][according][to][the][Riemannian][metric] _[g]_[,][and] _[∇][ξ][u]_[is][the] Riemannian covariant derivative.
comments.explanation: The displayed divergence formula is the standard one, and it is correct when interpreted pointwise using a local orthonormal frame. The possible issue is only in the wording: on a general orientable compact Riemannian manifold, a global orthonormal frame need not exist; orientability does not imply parallelizability. For example, S^2 is orientable but has no global nonvanishing frame. If the authors mean a local orthonormal frame near the point where the divergence is evaluated, then the formula is fine and independent of the chosen local frame. But a reader could reasonably read “an orthonormal frame” globally, which would add an unintended assumption on M. This is a small but real precision issue in a section emphasizing general manifolds.

comments.title: Using an MLP to parameterize a tangent vector field on a general manifold is underspecified
comments.quote: where _u ∈_ X( _M_ ) is the degree of freedom of the model. We model this degree of freedom, _u_ , with a deep neural network, more specifically, Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP).
comments.explanation: The modeling intent is clear: the learnable object is a smooth tangent vector field, and later sections give concrete constructions for Euclidean submanifolds and the flat torus. However, at this point the statement is made for a general compact Riemannian manifold M. A vanilla MLP does not by itself define a globally smooth section of TM: one must specify coordinates, an embedding and projection, a frame/trivialization, or an atlas with compatibility conditions, and tangency must be enforced. This is not merely an implementation detail on arbitrary manifolds, since many tangent bundles are nontrivial and cannot be represented globally by a single coordinate-valued vector field without further structure. The later submanifold construction resolves this for the main experimental setting, so the issue is localized, but the general statement is somewhat too terse and could mislead readers into thinking that an unconstrained MLP automatically yields an element of X(M).

comments.title: Theorem 2’s minimizer claim is not qualified by model-class representability
comments.quote: **Theorem 2.** _For λ ≥_ 1 _and sufficiently small ϵ >_ 0 _, the unique minimizer of the loss in equation 14 is µ_ ¯ = _µ._
comments.explanation: The algebra leading into this theorem appears sound: using Lemma 1 and the decomposition of \(\bar\mu\) into clipped positive and violation parts, the loss can indeed be rewritten as a generalized KL term plus \((\lambda-1)\int_M \bar\mu_-\,dV\), up to the target entropy constant. Also, if \(\epsilon<\min_M\mu\), then setting \(\bar\mu=\mu\) gives zero KL and zero violation. What remains unclear is the domain over which this minimization is being taken. In the surrounding text, \(\bar\mu\) is not an arbitrary density but is parameterized as \(\nu-\operatorname{div}(u)\), with \(u\) represented by a neural network. The proof only establishes the population optimum over an unrestricted class of admissible functions containing \(\mu\). It does not show that a finite MLP parameterization can realize \(\mu\) exactly; indeed, for merely continuous targets, exact representation by the divergence of a smooth neural vector field is not automatic. Later universality results can justify approximation, but not the exact unique minimizer statement as written. The theorem should be qualified as a statement about the unconstrained functional, or should explicitly assume that the model class contains a vector field yielding \(\bar\mu=\mu\).

comments.title: Closest-point projection is treated as a globally well-defined smooth map
comments.quote: We denote by _π_ : R _[d] →M_ the closest point projection on _M_ , i.e., _π_ ( _**x**_ ) = min _**y** ∈M ∥_ _**x** −_ _**y** ∥_ , with _∥_ _**y** ∥_[2] = _⟨_ _**y** ,_ _**y** ⟩_ the Euclidean norm in R _[d]_ .
comments.explanation: I understand the intended construction: define an ambient vector field by projecting an MLP output onto the tangent space at the closest point on the manifold, so that its restriction to \(M\) is tangent and locally constant along normal fibers. However, for a general compact embedded submanifold, the nearest-point projection is not globally single-valued or smooth; it is only guaranteed to be well-defined and smooth in a tubular neighborhood of \(M\). The displayed definition also writes a minimum distance rather than an argmin point, though that is likely a notation slip. This matters because equation 18 and the subsequent divergence lemma rely on differentiating through \(\pi\) and \(P_{\pi(x)}\). The construction can probably be repaired by restricting \(\pi\) to a tubular neighborhood and extending the field arbitrarily outside it, since the divergence is evaluated on \(M\). But as stated, the global map \(\pi:\mathbb R^d\to M\) is not generally available, which weakens the formal validity of the submanifold construction.

comments.title: Triangulated surfaces are listed although the subsequent machinery assumes smooth manifolds
comments.quote: Examples include implicit surfaces and manifolds (i.e., preimage of a regular value of a smooth function), as well as triangulated surfaces and manifold simplicial complexes.
comments.explanation: The inclusion of implicit regular level sets is consistent with the smooth submanifold assumptions used later: tangent spaces, normal projections, closest-point maps, and Riemannian divergence are all classical objects there. The mention of triangulated surfaces and manifold simplicial complexes is less clear. Such objects are generally only piecewise smooth and do not have a unique tangent plane or classical divergence at edges or vertices. The subsequent definitions of \(P_x\), \(N_xM\), \(\operatorname{div}(u)\), and Lemma 2 therefore do not apply directly without either assuming the triangulation represents an underlying smooth surface, smoothing it, or replacing the operators with discrete differential-geometric analogues. Since the experiments later use SDF-based surfaces, this may just be an informal motivation, but the statement could mislead readers about the scope of the method as formally developed.

comments.title: Generated density comparison is underspecified when the learned density violates positivity
comments.quote: Figure 3 shows, for different values of _λ_ , our density estimation _µ_ +, the push-forward density Φ _∗ν_ , and their absolute difference. To evaluate Φ _∗ν_ from the vector field _vt_ , we solve an ODE as advised in Grathwohl et al. (2018).
comments.explanation:
The intended experiment is clear: they want to check whether the explicit density estimate used during training agrees with the density obtained by actually integrating the Moser-flow ODE. Earlier theory indeed says these should agree when \( ar\mu\) is positive, because then the vector field \(v_t = u / ((1-t)
u + t ar\mu)\) realizes a valid CNF from \(
u\) to \( ar\mu\). What remains unclear is what exactly is done when \( ar\mu\) has negative regions, which is precisely the regime being probed by varying \(\lambda\). If the ODE uses \( ar\mu\) in the denominator, the interpolating density need not stay positive and the Moser construction is not guaranteed to define the desired flow. If instead it uses the clipped \( ar\mu_+\), then the divergence equation \( ar\mu=
u-\operatorname{div}u\) no longer matches the target density in the ODE, so the standard Moser argument does not imply that \(\Phi_*
u= ar\mu_+\). The figure’s interpretation depends on this implementation choice, but the passage only says “from the vector field \(v_t\)” and does not specify how \(v_t\) is constructed under clipping or positivity violations.

comments.title: Sampling on the extracted mesh is not clearly reconciled with the implicit-surface model
comments.quote: To generate uniform ( _ν_ ) and data ( _µ_ ) samples over _M_ we first extract a mesh _M[′]_ from _f_ using the Marching Cubes algorithm (Lorensen and Cline, 1987) setting its resolution to 100x100x100. Then, to randomly choose a point uniformly from _M[′]_ we first randomly choose a face of the mesh with probability proportional to its area, and then randomly choose a point uniformly within that face.
comments.explanation: Using a Marching Cubes mesh as a practical proxy for an implicit surface is reasonable, and the area-weighted face sampling is the standard way to sample uniformly from a triangle mesh. The concern is that the model and the divergence argument in Section 4 are formulated for points \(x\in M=f^{-1}(0)\), with tangent projections and SDF-based projection operators evaluated on the implicit surface. Points sampled uniformly inside faces of the polygonal mesh \(M'\) generally do not lie exactly on the smooth zero level set \(M\), even if the vertices approximate it. The passage says these are samples “over \(M\)” but does not state whether the sampled mesh points are projected back to the implicit surface before training/evaluation, or whether the mesh \(M'\) is treated as the actual manifold for the experiment. This matters because the tangent projection, divergence computation, and likelihood are defined with respect to the smooth implicit surface, not the approximate triangulation.

comments.title: Target density construction on the mesh may be missing area weighting
comments.quote: Next, we sampled the eigenfunctions at the faces’ centers, clamped their negative values, and normalized to get discrete probability densities over the faces of _M[′]_ . Then, to sample a point, we first choose a face at random based on this probability, and then random a point uniformly within that face.
comments.explanation: The intended construction seems to be a surface density proportional to the positive part of a Laplace–Beltrami eigenfunction. For a continuous density \(h\) with respect to surface area, the probability of a triangle face should be proportional to \(h(c_j)\,\mathrm{area}(F_j)\), not just to the clamped value \(h(c_j)\). The authors did explicitly mention area weighting for uniform sampling, so I checked whether the phrase “discrete probability densities over the faces” might implicitly include the face areas. It might, but the described sequence—sample eigenfunction values at face centers, clamp, normalize, then choose a face based on this probability—reads as if the values themselves are normalized over faces. If so, the induced continuous density within each face is inversely affected by triangle area and is not simply the clamped manifold harmonic density. At minimum, the area-weighting convention is underspecified, which affects reproducibility of the curved-surface experiment.

comments.title: Eigenfunction index is conflated with eigenvalue/frequency
comments.quote: we first computed the _k_ -th eigenfunction of the Laplace-Beltrami operator over _M[′]_ (we provide details on this computation in the supplementary), for the frequencies (eigenvalues) _k ∈{_ 10 _,_ 50 _,_ 500 _}_ .
comments.explanation: The beginning of the sentence suggests that \(k\) is an eigenfunction index: the 10th, 50th, and 500th eigenfunctions. That is a common and plausible experimental choice. But the parenthetical “frequencies (eigenvalues) \(k\in\{10,50,500\}\)” conflates this index with the actual Laplace–Beltrami eigenvalue, which is generally not an integer and depends on the mesh geometry and discretization. A careful reader can probably infer that the authors mean eigenfunction indices rather than literal eigenvalues, especially from “\(k\)-th eigenfunction,” but the wording is mathematically imprecise and could mislead someone trying to reproduce the exact target densities.

comments.title: Parametric methods are described as invertible maps, but the cited wrapped constructions are generally non-invertible
comments.quote: Parametric methods consist of a normalizing flow in the Euclidean space R _[n]_ , pushed-forward onto the manifold through an invertible map _ψ_ : R _[n] →M_ . However, to globally represent the manifold, _ψ_ needs to be a homeomorphism implying that _M_ and R _[n]_ are topologically equivalent, limiting the scope of that approach. Existing methods in this class are often based on the exponential map exp _x_ : _TxM_ = _[∼]_ R _[n] →M_ of a manifold. This leads to the so called _wrapped_ distributions.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended distinction: methods that use a Euclidean latent/tangent parameterization of the manifold face global topological obstructions, whereas Riemannian methods work intrinsically. The homeomorphism point is also mathematically standard if one wants a single global bijective chart from R^n to M. What remains confusing is that the paragraph first defines the class via an invertible map ψ, but then treats exponential-map wrapped distributions as instances of the same setup. On compact manifolds such as spheres or SO(3), the exponential map is not globally injective, and precisely because of this the wrapped density is computed by summing over multiple preimages. Thus these examples do not satisfy the stated “invertible map” formulation. A reader could reasonably wonder whether the criticism applies to bijective parametrizations, to many-to-one wrapped maps, or to both. The fix would be to separate globally invertible Euclidean parametrizations from non-injective exponential-map wrapping methods.

comments.title: Riemannian methods are said to avoid mapping-induced numerical instabilities too broadly
comments.quote: In contrast to Parametric methods, Riemannian methods operate directly on the manifold itself and, as such, avoid numerical instabilities that arise from the mapping onto the manifold.
comments.explanation: The intended contrast is clear and mostly fair: intrinsic or manifold-defined flows avoid relying on a single global Euclidean-to-manifold parametrization, so they avoid some of the singularities and topological problems of chart/exponential-map approaches. However, the statement is broader than what is generally true. Several Riemannian flow constructions still use exponential maps, logarithmic maps, retractions, coordinate charts, projections, or manifold-specific numerical ODE solvers, each of which can introduce numerical issues. The same paragraph immediately mentions a geodesic/exponential-map-based method, and the paper’s own embedded-surface implementation uses projection machinery. So the issue is not that Riemannian methods avoid mapping-related instabilities categorically, but that they avoid the particular global-parametrization instability of Euclidean push-forward approaches. This is a mild overstatement rather than a flaw in the method.

comments.title: Claim that limiting the flow space does not limit generated distributions needs the theorem’s qualifiers
comments.quote: Moser Flow also generates a CNF, however by limiting the flow space (albeit, not the generated distributions) it allows expressing the learned distribution as the divergence of a vector field.
comments.explanation: I read this as referring to the paper’s central point: Moser’s construction restricts the time-dependent vector field to a special form, yet still gives universal density modeling under the assumptions proved earlier. That is a reasonable intuition, and the paper does provide universality results for positive continuous target densities on compact boundaryless manifolds/submanifolds, up to approximation by the neural parametrization. Still, the parenthetical “not the generated distributions” is stated without those qualifications. It could be read as an exact, unrestricted expressivity claim over all distributions or all CNF pushforwards, whereas the established result is for positive densities, under compactness/orientability/boundary assumptions, and in the neural case in an approximation sense. Adding “under the assumptions of our universality theorem” or “up to arbitrary approximation for positive continuous densities” would make the claim precise.
